item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We can see that it hit other planets, or something similair did, what if we are next? Don't let it go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had an initial disinterest in this due to the use of [DATA EXPUNGED], but the deliberation of the Committee v. the Council really did justify the in-universe purpose of the expungements. Great work +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd give two +1s. One for this SCP and one for this guy.

Despite how many varitions of materials consisted to build this intergalactic planet-obliterating nuke, if by any chance we found a way to obtain the shell of it, we can repurpose it as a containment chamber to contain whatever moves so goddamn fast.

And according to your calculations, we can make 100cm diameter frisbees using this material with similar mass of normal 15-20cm frisbees which is around 350 grams. Your dog would enjoy playing this SCP frisbee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi! I did some math because I'm a nitpicking piece of shit.

A cyllinder with dimensions of 155m/30m would be about 1.1*10^5 cubic meters of matter. The equivalent of one kilogram of mass is 8.99*10^16 joules (or almost 90 petajoules).
Now, if the collision produces over 1YJ, that puts the relativistic mass at 1.11*10^7 kg, and thusly the zero-velocity mass at 3.47×10^6.
Is the anomaly's extremely low density of 31.54 kg per cubic meter a part of it? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. The expunges, despite being numerous, don't obscure the core idea of the article, and the ethical question it raises is an interesting one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems like attempting to destroy it would be risky due to its self-repair functions. And they can't just drop it into the sun (even under controlled circumstances) since the missile could potentially destroy it, or do some other form if irreparable damage. The best chance of neutralizing it without either Earth OR its target being destroyed is to find a world without intelligent life (preferably a barren rogue planet) and activate it there. NOT an easy task.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does anything in particular prevent them from attaching something to the device after it's left Earth, or influencing its trajectory once it's away from Earth, to keep it from hitting its target?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty close
close

The O5's don't care about what happens to the planet, they just want to get rid of SCP-3320. They are more worried about being detected by whoever created SCP-3320, since they have a tendency to go around blowing up other planets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay so I think I know what's going on here.
Am I correct?

SCP-3320 is a device intended to destroy SCP-3320-1 (which is also implied to be the homeworld of SCP-163 according to the e-mail and the description of the species engraved onto SCP-3320). The O5s know this and want the planet destroyed too as a precaution against them detecting Earth and causing a shitstorm in the future. The Ethics Committee is at odds with the Council on this, seeing no need to destroy a planet that is causing no harm to the human race.

item.title: 'Ethical Relativity'
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to TheGreatSea for reading the draft.

Image source.
